title: How a night market organises itself
summary: Explain how a sprawling night market arranges its stalls, prices, and crowd flow without any planner in charge — and what that quiet self-organisation teaches us about order that no one designs.
topic: Why a night market runs smoothly even though no one is in charge of it
prompt: Write a 450–600-word explanatory essay on how a busy night market organises its layout, pricing, and crowd movement without a central planner, and what this reveals about order that emerges rather than being imposed. Give two concrete examples and concede one objection. Use cleft sentences, the passive voice, and complex concession (although / even though).
hintStarters: What looks like chaos from the entrance turns out, on a second look, to be…
Although no committee assigned the stalls their places, the layout follows a logic that…
It is repetition, not regulation, that teaches each vendor where the…
Even though prices are never published, two stalls selling the same dish somehow…

hintVocab.meaningVi: một người lập kế hoạch trung tâm
một sự sắp xếp bất thành văn
bán phá giá / hạ giá hơn đối thủ
chỗ bày hàng / vị trí bán
những quy tắc phi chính thức
lối đi chính / trục giao thông
ổn định vào một khuôn mẫu

sampleText:
Arrive at a large night market in Taipei or Marrakesh at dusk and the first impression is of pure disorder. Hundreds of vendors are unfolding tables, lighting burners, and shouting over one another; the crowd pours in from three directions at once. No one stands at the gate with a clipboard assigning anyone a place. Yet by nine o'clock the same market is running like a body that knows its own anatomy, and almost everything that makes it work was decided by no one in particular. What looks like chaos from the entrance turns out, on a second look, to be order of a kind that planners can rarely build on purpose.

Consider first where the stalls end up. Although no committee assigned the stalls their places, the layout follows a logic that has been shaped over many evenings. The fruit sellers gather near the entrance, where browsers are still deciding; the grilled-meat stalls take the smoky middle, where the smell does their advertising; the dessert and drink stands wait at the far end, where a full stomach starts wanting something sweet. Each vendor chose a pitch for selfish reasons, and the sum of those choices is a route that pulls a hungry crowd from savoury to sweet without a single sign telling it to move. It is repetition, not regulation, that teaches each vendor where the money walks.

Prices behave the same way. Even though prices are never published, two stalls selling the same skewer somehow land within a coin of each other by the end of the week. A vendor who charges too much watches the queue form next door; one who charges too little is asked, gently, by neighbours, what he thinks he is doing. The price is not set by anyone, but it is discovered by everyone, nudged into place by a thousand small comparisons made by customers who walk on rather than argue. The figure that results is rarely far from fair, and no clerk ever calculated it.

Even crowd flow sorts itself out. A stall that draws a long queue would block the lane, so its owner learns, after a few crushed evenings, to angle the table and serve from the side. The slow bottleneck is fixed not by a rule but by the discomfort of everyone caught in it, which presses on the vendor until the geometry changes. The market is, in this sense, constantly editing itself, and the editor is the crowd.

Granted, this picture can be romanticised. Real markets are not perfectly fair: the best pitches are often held by whoever arrived years ago or pays a quiet fee to a local strongman, and a newcomer with a better dish may be kept at the dark end for a season. Power and history leave their marks here as everywhere, and spontaneous order is not the same as just order. The objection is sound and should temper any easy admiration.

Even so, what survives the objection is striking. A system with no designer, no map, and no published prices still manages to feed thousands of people, match supply to appetite, and unclog its own pathways night after night. The lesson is not that planning is useless but that some order is grown rather than drawn — assembled from countless small adjustments by people minding their own stalls — and that a planner who ignores this living kind of order will keep designing markets that feel, oddly, more dead than the messy ones that organise themselves.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: It-cleft to contrast the real teacher with the assumed one
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It is repetition, not regulation, that teaches each vendor where the money walks' dùng it-cleft với cặp đối lập 'X, not Y' để khẳng định rằng sự lặp lại (chứ không phải quy định) mới là thứ định hình hành vi.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is repetition, not regulation, that teaches each vendor where the money walks' uses an it-cleft with the 'X, not Y' contrast to assert that repetition, not regulation, shapes behaviour.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Concession + passive to show order without an author
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Although no committee assigned the stalls their places, …' nhượng bộ bằng 'although', còn 'The price is not set by anyone, but it is discovered by everyone' dùng bị động để cho thấy trật tự nảy sinh mà không có tác giả nào.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Although no committee assigned the stalls their places, …' concedes with 'although', while 'The price is not set by anyone, but it is discovered by everyone' uses the passive to show order arising with no single author.

sampleTranslationVi:
Hãy đến một khu chợ đêm lớn ở Đài Bắc hay Marrakesh vào lúc chạng vạng và ấn tượng đầu tiên là sự hỗn loạn thuần túy. Hàng trăm người bán đang mở bàn, đốt bếp, và hò hét chồng lên nhau; đám đông tràn vào từ ba hướng cùng một lúc. Không ai đứng ở cổng với một tấm bảng kẹp phân chỗ cho ai cả. Vậy mà đến chín giờ, chính khu chợ ấy vận hành như một cơ thể biết rõ giải phẫu của chính mình, và gần như mọi thứ khiến nó vận hành đều không do một ai cụ thể quyết định. Cái trông như hỗn loạn từ lối vào, nhìn lại lần nữa, hóa ra lại là một thứ trật tự mà các nhà quy hoạch hiếm khi dựng nên được một cách có chủ ý.

Hãy xét trước hết các quầy hàng rốt cuộc dừng lại ở đâu. Dù không hội đồng nào phân chỗ cho các quầy hàng, cách bố trí lại tuân theo một logic được định hình qua nhiều buổi tối. Những người bán trái cây tụ lại gần lối vào, nơi khách còn đang đắn đo; những quầy thịt nướng chiếm lấy phần giữa đầy khói, nơi mùi hương làm thay việc quảng cáo cho họ; những quầy tráng miệng và đồ uống chờ ở tận cuối, nơi một cái bụng no bắt đầu thèm chút ngọt. Mỗi người bán chọn một chỗ vì những lý do ích kỷ của riêng mình, và tổng của những lựa chọn ấy là một lộ trình kéo đám đông đói bụng từ món mặn sang món ngọt mà chẳng cần một tấm biển nào bảo nó di chuyển. Chính sự lặp lại, chứ không phải quy định, mới dạy cho mỗi người bán biết tiền đi qua lối nào.

Giá cả cũng hành xử y như vậy. Dù giá chẳng bao giờ được niêm yết, hai quầy bán cùng một xiên nướng bằng cách nào đó lại chốt ở mức chỉ chênh nhau một đồng xu vào cuối tuần. Người bán đắt quá nhìn thấy hàng người xếp hàng hình thành ở bên cạnh; người bán rẻ quá thì bị hàng xóm hỏi, nhẹ nhàng, rằng anh ta nghĩ mình đang làm gì vậy. Giá không do ai định ra, mà được mọi người khám phá ra, được đẩy nhẹ vào đúng chỗ bởi cả ngàn phép so sánh nhỏ do những khách hàng chọn bước tiếp thay vì tranh cãi. Con số rốt cuộc hiện ra hiếm khi xa rời sự công bằng, và chẳng nhân viên nào từng tính ra nó.

Ngay cả dòng người cũng tự sắp xếp lấy. Một quầy hút một hàng dài người xếp hàng sẽ làm nghẽn lối đi, nên chủ của nó, sau vài buổi tối chen chúc, học được cách xoay nghiêng cái bàn và phục vụ từ bên cạnh. Điểm tắc nghẽn chậm chạp được giải quyết không phải bởi một quy định mà bởi sự khó chịu của tất cả những người mắc kẹt trong đó, áp lực ấy đè lên người bán cho đến khi hình thể thay đổi. Theo nghĩa này, khu chợ liên tục tự biên tập chính mình, và biên tập viên chính là đám đông.

Công nhận, bức tranh này có thể bị lãng mạn hóa. Các khu chợ thật không hề công bằng tuyệt đối: những vị trí đẹp nhất thường do người đến từ nhiều năm trước nắm giữ hoặc do người trả một khoản phí ngầm cho một tay anh chị địa phương, và một người mới với món ngon hơn có thể bị giữ ở góc tối suốt một mùa. Quyền lực và lịch sử để lại dấu vết ở đây cũng như ở mọi nơi, và trật tự tự phát không giống với trật tự công bằng. Phản đối này có cơ sở và nên làm dịu bớt bất kỳ sự ngưỡng mộ dễ dãi nào.

Dẫu vậy, cái sống sót qua được phản đối thật đáng kinh ngạc. Một hệ thống không người thiết kế, không bản đồ, không giá niêm yết vẫn xoay xở nuôi sống hàng ngàn người, khớp cung với cơn thèm, và tự khơi thông những lối đi của mình hết đêm này sang đêm khác. Bài học không phải là quy hoạch vô dụng mà là có những trật tự được nuôi lớn lên chứ không phải được vẽ ra — được ráp lại từ vô số điều chỉnh nhỏ bởi những người chỉ lo cho quầy hàng của riêng mình — và rằng một nhà quy hoạch phớt lờ thứ trật tự sống động này sẽ cứ tiếp tục thiết kế ra những khu chợ mà, lạ thay, lại chết chóc hơn cả những khu chợ lộn xộn tự tổ chức lấy.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an it-cleft to name the real force shaping vendor behaviour?
options: It is repetition, not regulation, that teaches each vendor where the money walks.
Repetition teaches each vendor where the money walks, not the regulation does it.
Teaching each vendor is what repetition the money walks regulation does.
The money walks repetition not regulation it is that vendor each teaches where.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X, not Y, that …' is an it-cleft stressing repetition over regulation.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'It is X, not Y, that …' frame.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence opens with an 'although' concession and continues naturally?
options: Although no committee assigned the stalls, but the layout follows a logic over evenings shaped.
Although no committee assigned the stalls their places, the layout follows a logic shaped over many evenings.
No committee assigned the stalls although the layout shaped a logic follows evenings many.
Although the stalls places their no committee assigned, follows logic the layout shaped evenings.
mcQuestions.explanation: The 'Although …, …' clause concedes the absence of a planner and then states the main point cleanly.
mcQuestions.hint: A clause starting with 'although' should not be followed by 'but'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive to show the price has no single author?
options: Everyone discovers the price although no one really sets it themselves alone.
Setting the price is what everyone no one discovers does it.
The price is not set by anyone, but it is discovered by everyone.
By everyone discovered it by no one set the price is but anyone.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'is not set by anyone … is discovered by everyone' uses the passive to deny a single author.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option pairs two passive verbs to contrast 'no one' with 'everyone'?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'spontaneous order' mean as the essay uses it?
options: Order created by a government committee
A sudden burst of chaos
A printed map of stall positions
Order that arises on its own from many independent choices, with no central designer
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay describes order that emerges from countless individual decisions rather than being planned.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of order that grows rather than being drawn.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence concedes the fairness objection most honestly?
options: Granted, this picture can be romanticised; the best pitches are often held by whoever arrived years ago.
Markets are always perfectly fair to everyone who shows up.
Obviously corruption never touches any market anywhere.
Anyone who complains about market fairness is simply lazy.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' honestly concedes that power and history make the order less than fair.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option admits the order is not always just?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option hedges the admiration appropriately?
options: Spontaneous order is always perfectly fair and beyond criticism.
Spontaneous order is not the same as just order, and the objection should temper any easy admiration.
Spontaneous order has no flaws of any kind ever.
Order spontaneous fair just admiration temper objection always perfect.
mcQuestions.explanation: Distinguishing 'spontaneous' from 'just' and 'tempering admiration' hedges the praise carefully.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option that limits how much to admire the system.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, how does a stall fix a queue that blocks the lane?
options: A market official orders it to move
The queue is banned by a written rule
Its owner learns to angle the table and serve from the side after crowded evenings
The stall is forced to close early
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says the discomfort of the crowd presses the vendor to change the geometry of the stall.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps a consistent explanatory register?
options: Honestly night markets are super fun and the food is amazing, right?
Basically the stalls just end up wherever, simple as that, who cares.
It's kind of wild how, like, nobody's in charge but it works, you know?
The market is, in this sense, constantly editing itself, and the editor is the crowd.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B is precise and analytical; the others slip into casual speech.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots from the fairness objection back to the main point?
options: Even so, what survives the objection is striking: a system with no designer still feeds thousands.
Anyway, fairness is the only thing that matters in any market.
In conclusion, planning is always better than letting things organise themselves.
On the contrary, no market has ever fed anyone.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' accepts the objection and then advances the essay's central observation.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase accepts the prior point and reasserts the explanation?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to undercut a price' mean?
options: To raise a price above a rival's
To sell at a lower price than a competitor to win customers
To refuse to sell at any price
To publish a price on a sign
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Undercut' means to charge less than a competitor in order to attract buyers.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of beating a rival by going cheaper.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses parallel structure most effectively?
options: unfolding tables, to light burners, and they shout over one another
unfold tables, the burners lighting, and shouted over another one
unfolding tables, lighting burners, and shouting over one another
tables unfolding, light the burners, and one another shout over they
mcQuestions.explanation: Three matched '-ing' phrases form a clean parallel list.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps all three verbs in the same '-ing' form?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's main lesson about order?
options: That planning is always useless and should be abandoned
That night markets are too chaotic to function
That only a central planner can create real order
That some order is grown from countless small adjustments rather than drawn by a designer
mcQuestions.explanation: The conclusion distinguishes order that is grown from below from order that is drawn from above.

options: A point where movement is squeezed and slowed
A type of glass container
A bargain price
The entrance to the market
mcQuestions.explanation: A 'bottleneck' is a narrow point where the flow of people gets stuck.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of where a crowd gets jammed up.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best captures the essay's nuanced conclusion?
options: Planners should never design any public space at all.
A planner who ignores grown order will keep designing markets that feel oddly more dead than the messy ones that organise themselves.
Self-organising markets are always superior in every possible way.
Night markets prove that rules are completely unnecessary in life.
mcQuestions.explanation: The conclusion warns planners against ignoring the living, bottom-up order markets reveal.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option that warns planners, not the one that dismisses planning entirely.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an em-dash insertion correctly to add a defining clause?
options: some order grown rather — assembled drawn the countless — adjustments small a planner stalls dead markets ignores.
grown rather than drawn — some order is — by people the adjustments minding countless dead small a planner.
some order is grown rather than drawn — assembled from countless small adjustments by people minding their own stalls — and a planner who ignores it designs dead markets
a planner dead markets — some order grown — drawn rather assembled stalls minding countless designs it ignores.
mcQuestions.explanation: The dashes enclose a defining phrase; remove them and the main sentence remains grammatical.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option lets you delete the dashed phrase and keep a full sentence?

options: The sound of footsteps at night
A rule about where to walk
A type of street performance
The flow of people walking through a place
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Foot traffic' is the movement of pedestrians through an area, which vendors read to choose pitches.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of how many people walk past a spot.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a system in your own life — a shared kitchen, a group chat, a team workflow — that runs on unwritten rules no one ever decided. If a manager tried to formalise every one of those rules into policy, would the system work better, or would it quietly stop working the way it does now?
